nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, sheltered tropical marine environments; intertidal and subtidal zones on rocky surfaces with sand cover
nativeRange.biotope: Marine benthic; anchors via cylindrical stolons to sand and rockwork in well-lit shallow reef flats and lagoons

avoidWith: herbivorous fish (tangs, rabbitfish, angelfish, sea urchins)

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon, 1200x913. Sharp in-situ specimen: forked sawtooth-edged mint-green blades rising from a clearly visible cylindrical stolon — textbook diagnostic C. serrulata form; single clean subject.
Thomas Irvine, 2048x1463. Tack-sharp close macro clearly resolving the serrated (sawtooth) margins on the flat forked blades — strong diagnostic detail; slight clutter from adjacent green sprigs.
Debra Baker, 2048x1536. Sharp dense colony mat showing the serrated blade texture and mint-green colour across a healthy spread.

summary: Cactus Tree Caulerpa (Caulerpa serrulata) produces small, forked, sawtooth-edged blades rising from creeping stems, giving it a distinctive branching silhouette. Generally considered hardy and reef-tolerant, though like all Caulerpa it requires careful management to prevent overgrowth, chemical stress to corals, and reproductive crashes.
careNotes:
Cactus Tree Caulerpa (Caulerpa serrulata) is a siphonous green macroalga that produces cylindrical, creeping stems (stolons) from which small, forked, sawtooth-edged blades — roughly 3 cm each — grow upward in a branching pattern. The coloring is generally a light to medium mint green, often with a slight bluish tint, and the overall spread of a healthy colony has been reported at roughly 10–30 cm. The species is generally considered hardy and easy to keep, suited to display use where the serrated blade form adds visual texture, as well as refugium use for nutrient export.

DISTINCT FROM SIBLINGS: This is the true 'Cactus Tree' species and is distinct from Caulerpa cupressoides (sw-algae-025 'Cactus Caulerpa', which has the trade name 'Cactus Tree Caulerpa' as an alias) and from Caulerpa subserrulata (sw-algae-064 'Mini Serrated Caulerpa'). Confirm the scientific name before purchase, as trade names overlap heavily across the genus.

LIGHTING & FLOW: Moderate-to-high, full-spectrum lighting is generally recommended; actinic-only light is not sufficient for healthy growth. Low to moderate water flow tends to work best, as excessive current can tear the delicate blades.

ALLELOPATHY: Like all Caulerpa species, C. serrulata is generally considered to release secondary metabolites (caulerpin, caulerpenyne, and sesquiterpenes) into the water column. These compounds have been reported to stress nearby corals and competing algae. Physical separation from sensitive SPS corals and the use of activated carbon in the system are commonly recommended precautions. The species tends to be left alone by most algae-eating fish, as its stiff blade structure makes it less palatable than softer algae.

GOING SEXUAL / REPRODUCTIVE CRASH: All Caulerpa species can undergo holocarpy — a mass reproductive event in which the organism converts its cytoplasm into gametes and effectively dies, releasing organic matter into the water column. This can cause an acute water-quality crash. Standard mitigations: run refugium lighting on an uninterrupted 24-hour photoperiod to suppress reproductive triggering; harvest regularly to keep the colony in vegetative growth; maintain stable nutrient levels; promptly remove any specimen showing reproductive structures (translucent or white fronds, or a turbid release into the water).

INVASIVE CONCERN: Caulerpa species are potentially invasive and must never be released into natural waterways. Standard disposal: bag the algae, freeze for 24+ hours, then discard in regular trash. Caulerpa taxifolia (NOT this species) is federally noxious in the US; C. serrulata is not listed federally, but California maintains a precautionary ban on Caulerpa shipments.

MICRONUTRIENTS: Weekly dosing of iron, iodine, and other trace elements has been commonly recommended for sustained macroalgae health.
breedingNotes: Propagates in the aquarium primarily via fragmentation — cuttings of the stolon re-anchor and resume vegetative growth — and by vegetative tip growth along the stolon.

primary.name: Reefer's Den — Caulerpa serrulata (Sawtooth Algae)
primary.notes: AlgaeBarn and Mosaic Macros do not currently carry C. serrulata (AlgaeBarn search returned no results; Mosaic search returned 38 unrelated items). Reefer's Den used as primary trade-source substitute: provides lighting (moderate), flow (low-to-moderate), size (~5 in / 12.5 cm), placement (refugium and display), care level (easy), growth rate (moderate), propagation (fragmentation, sporulation), and palatability notes. Temperature 78–86°F = ~25.6–30°C noted but flagged high-side.

additional.name: Reeflings — Caulerpa serrulata care guide
additional.notes: Secondary care reference. Confirms water parameters (temp 22–28°C / 72–82°F, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), moderate-to-high lighting, anchorage requirement, native range (Caribbean, Indo-Pacific, Red Sea), and rapid growth potential requiring pruning. Averaged temperature with Reefer's Den (~25°C upper) yields final 22–27°C in waterParameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Caulerpa serrulata
additional.notes: Taxonomy verified: Family Caulerpaceae, Order Bryopsidales, Class Ulvophyceae, Division Chlorophyta. Common names ('cactus tree alga', 'serrated green seaweed') confirmed. Morphology (~30 cm thallus, ~3 cm forked serrated blades, light-to-medium mint green with bluish tints) and native range (Caribbean: south Florida, Bahamas; Western Australia, India, Singapore, southeast Asia, East Africa) confirmed. Depth to ~5 m on rocky surfaces with sand cover; we extend max to 15 m to align with sibling Caulerpa entries and Reefer's Den's 1–15 ft (0.3–4.5 m) wild collection depth.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary — authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm), 4-method propagation standard (superglue, zip-tie/fishing line, wedging, sand-bed — Caulerpa accepts all 4), allelopathy/crash convention for Caulerpa, micronutrient dosing guidance, and lighting/flow defaults.
